# **Architectural Simulation and Economic Automation: A Reverse-Engineering Analysis of Dealer Mechanics in Incremental Frameworks**

## **1\. Introduction: Contextualizing Automated Liquidation in Digital Economies**

The evolution of browser-based economic simulations, particularly within the subgenre of incremental and idle games, represents a continuous refinement of resource management paradigms. Early iterations of these digital ecosystems relied heavily on active, localized player inputs, often manifesting as repetitive clicking mechanics designed to simulate manual labor and direct resource generation. However, subsequent architectural developments sought to transition the user experience from manual execution to overarching macro-management. An examination of the application framework in question provides a definitive blueprint for how automated resource liquidation functions within a localized, continuously scaling digital economy.1

Originally prototyped over a period of two weeks utilizing the AngularJS structural framework and Bootstrap for user interface design 1, the simulation bypasses active clicking mechanics entirely.1 The developer explicitly noted that such repetitive interactions were tedious, opting instead to build the core gameplay loop around a passive human-resource management system.1 The fundamental architecture centers on generating illicit commodities—ranging from organic agricultural products like cannabis and mushrooms to highly synthesized chemical compounds such as methamphetamine and lysergic acid diethylamide 1—and subsequently establishing an automated pipeline to liquidate these assets into liquid capital.

This liquidation pipeline is entirely dependent on autonomous converter entities, designated within the application as "dealers." This report provides an exhaustive, granular deconstruction of the dealer mechanic. The analysis focuses specifically on the underlying mathematical algorithms governing margin (price per unit) and volume (throughput capacity), the bivariate visual star rating system, and the state-management framework required to process these variables in real time. The ultimate objective is to extract, analyze, and formalize these economic mechanics so that the exact structural dependencies governing the relationship between star ratings, gram measurements, and monetary yield can be effectively ported and integrated into external proprietary game development architectures.

## **2\. Technical Infrastructure and Application State Management**

Before deconstructing the economic formulas governing price and volume, it is crucial to understand the technical substrate that drives the game's data processing and user interface rendering. The application is built upon AngularJS, a JavaScript-based structural framework that fundamentally relies on two-way data binding to synchronize the graphical user interface with the underlying logical data models automatically.3 Understanding this structural dependency is critical for developers seeking to implement similar logic in modern engines.

### **2.1 Scope Architecture and Master Data Models**

The primary state of the economic simulation is maintained within a centralized master object typically instantiated in the controller scope, denoted within the application's syntax as gameModel.3 Within this overarching object hierarchy, specialized arrays such as gameModel.drugs and gameModel.dealers store the real-time, mutable attributes of the player's production facilities and hired sales assets.3 Because AngularJS dynamically evaluates expressions directly within the Document Object Model on a continuous $digest cycle, the mathematical formulas calculating financial yield and product throughput are executed persistently.

The HTML DOM structure reveals the implementation of localized rendering scopes using the ng-repeat directive. The specific code block \<div class="col-xs-12 dealer-element" ng-repeat="dealer in gameModel.dealers | orderBy:dealerSort:dealerSortReverse track by $index"\> demonstrates that the rendering engine iterates through the dynamic array of currently hired automation units.3 The presence of dynamic sorting filters (orderBy:dealerSort:dealerSortReverse) highlights the necessity for players to rapidly organize their workforce based on immediate economic needs, toggling the view to prioritize units based on their underlying value for either maximum volume throughput or maximum profit margins.3

### **2.2 User Interface Discoverability and Deliberate Data Obfuscation**

A highly sophisticated design choice implemented within this simulation is the intentional obfuscation of raw mathematical performance data. The user interface explicitly prioritizes heuristic visual indicators over absolute numerical integers. The application utilizes a custom interpolation function, visible in the source code as getStars(dealer.originalVolume, 5\) and getStars(dealer.originalPrice, 5), to render a graphical representation of an individual unit's competency on a universally understood scale.3

The absolute values governing the exact gram-per-second yield and the precise monetary return per gram are hidden within hidden HTML properties, specifically leveraging customized tooltip classes (jg-tooltip left-tip).3 The source code reveals that these tooltips dynamically generate strings such as {{dealer.name.split(' ')}} can sell up to {{actualDealerVolume(dealer,dealer.drug)|weight}} of {{dealer.drug}} per second.3 This design philosophy effectively bridges the gap between varying player typologies. Casual users can rely solely on the visual five-star ratings to make rapid, intuitive hiring decisions. Conversely, players focused on deep algorithmic optimization are required to actively discover the tooltips to extract the precise formulas outputting dealer.cashPerSecond, thereby enabling them to calculate perfect localized supply-chain equilibriums.1

Furthermore, the integration of grid system classes such as col-xs-12 and col-sm-9, along with adaptive directives like ng-class="{'col-lg-6' : options.kongregateLargeWindow}", indicates that the entire statistical rendering framework was designed to scale dynamically across varying screen resolutions, accommodating both mobile devices and specialized web portal viewports.3 This robust visual scaling ensures the complex economic data remains legible regardless of the platform.

## **3\. The Automation Paradigm: Dealers as Autonomous Resource Converters**

In standard macroeconomic simulations, the conversion of a manufactured commodity into liquid capital requires an intermediary logistical throughput mechanism. Within the specific parameters of this application, this mechanism is personified entirely by the "dealer" entity. These units act as localized, autonomous resource converters that draw continuously from a centralized, global inventory of commodities and convert them into usable currency based on strictly predefined, mathematically capped limits.

### **3.1 Unit Acquisition, Market Churn, and Hiring Friction**

The application employs a deliberately frictionless employment market to stimulate continuous player interaction in the absence of a clicking mechanic. Players are granted the ability to hire and terminate these autonomous units at will without incurring any direct, upfront financial penalties for the administrative transaction itself.1 This structural framework creates a randomized sub-game wherein the player is encouraged to continually churn through a procedurally generated pool of potential hires to locate optimal combinations of performance statistics. The frictionless nature of this system fundamentally shifts the economic calculus; the true "cost" of employing a dealer is not a static acquisition fee, but rather the ongoing opportunity cost of retaining a suboptimal unit that fails to maximize either the financial margin or the throughput velocity of a specific commodity production pipeline.1

The psychological weight of this system is emphasized by user feedback indicating the severe consequences of accidentally terminating highly optimized units. When a player inadvertently fires a top-tier unit—such as an entity possessing a maximum volume rating alongside an anomalous over-scaled price rating—they permanently lose not only the unit's intrinsic base statistical multipliers but also all of the localized capital previously invested into that unit's specific progression upgrades.1 This permanent data destruction underscores the absolute necessity of implementing rigorous interface fail-safes, such as secondary confirmation dialogues, when designing equivalent high-stakes data manipulation systems in external projects.1

### **3.2 Environmental Risks and Asynchronous Status Modifiers**

To prevent the automation economy from scaling infinitely without any requirement for active hazard mitigation, the simulation introduces localized, stochastic environmental disruptions. The units do not operate within an entirely sterile vacuum; they are subject to simulated law enforcement interventions. The application's logic architecture includes persistent conditional flags such as dealer.arrested, which interfaces directly with the user interface via conditional rendering blocks: \<p class="... dealer-arrest-message" ng-show="dealer.arrested && options.hideTop"\>{{dealer.arrestMessage}}\</p\>.3

When a simulated policing event triggers against a specific automation node, the unit's functionality is temporarily paralyzed, ceasing all automated conversion of goods into cash and bringing that specific sector of the supply chain to a halt.1 The player is immediately presented with a localized economic dilemma: endure the temporal penalty until the unit's status flag is automatically reverted to an active state, or expend an immediate, steep financial premium, conceptually framed as a "bail" payment of fifty thousand dollars, to instantly restore the unit's throughput capabilities.1 This mechanic effectively functions as a systemic tax on exponential growth, requiring the player to maintain emergency liquidity reserves rather than reinvesting one hundred percent of their capital directly back into production infrastructure.

## **4\. Deconstructing the Bivariate Valuation System: The Star Mechanics**

The defining characteristic of an individual automation unit within this simulation is its overarching statistical proficiency, which is quantified and presented to the user via a bifurcated visual rating system. Units are graded on a visual scale across two distinctly independent vectors: Volume and Margin, the latter frequently referred to simply as "Price" within the application's source syntax.1 While the standard interface implies a maximum rating of five stars, empirical evidence derived from the application's user base indicates that extreme statistical anomalies and progression retention mechanics can yield units possessing up to a six-star rating in specific categories.1

### **4.1 The Independent Functions of the Primary Metrics**

To successfully extract and map this data architecture into an independent, proprietary project, the exact functional separation of these two variables must be completely understood. They operate on entirely different axes of the overarching economic equation.

The Margin metric, heavily integrated via the dealer.originalPrice base variable, strictly determines the absolute monetary value extracted per single standardized unit of commodity sold, which the game measures in grams.3 A unit possessing a high-margin rating operates with extreme financial efficiency, generating the maximum theoretically possible revenue from the absolute minimum amount of product consumed.1

Conversely, the Volume metric, integrated via the dealer.originalVolume base variable, dictates the raw logistical throughput capacity of the autonomous unit.3 It defines the absolute maximum mass of a commodity that the unit can successfully process, remove from the global inventory pipeline, and liquidate per temporal cycle, measured as seconds.1

### **4.2 Economic Oscillation and Temporal Mathematical Dominance**

The continuous interplay between these two disparate statistics forms the foundational strategic layer of the economic loop. The systemic data clearly indicates that the utility of these statistics is inversely proportional to one another depending on the macro-level stage of the player's economic development.1

During the incipient stages of the simulation, the player's underlying production infrastructure is inherently weak and inefficient. The mathematical rate of commodity generation per second is significantly lower than the baseline throughput capacity of even an average, low-tier dealer. Because the overall inventory pipeline is severely bottlenecked by the lack of raw materials, a high-volume dealer is rendered entirely mathematically inefficient. A unit may possess the abstract capacity to process and sell ten grams of material per second, but if the agricultural facilities are only generating a single gram per second, the excess capacity is wasted computational potential. Therefore, optimal early-game strategic execution heavily favors units with maximum Margin stars.1 If the entire infrastructure can only support the processing of a single gram, the player must ensure that the specific gram yields the highest possible financial return multiplier.

However, as the player inevitably reinvests capital into exponential production upgrades, the rate of commodity generation will eventually eclipse the baseline throughput capacity of high-margin automation nodes. The user interface anticipates this critical inversion point and explicitly tracks the impending bottleneck. It utilizes a conditional logical evaluation, checking if drugSoldPerSecond(drug) \>= drugMadePerSecond(drug), to dynamically render a visual warning icon: \<span class="glyphicon glyphicon-warning-sign pull-left"...\>.3

When the visual warning persists, it indicates that inventory is accumulating significantly faster than it can be liquidated. At this inflection point, the absolute limitation on macro-economic growth ceases to be the profit margin and becomes pure logistical throughput. The player is forced to execute a major administrative restructuring, terminating highly efficient high-margin units and replacing them with high-volume units specifically to clear the massive logistical backlog and restore capital liquidity. This oscillation between prioritizing margin and prioritizing volume is the primary psychological driver preventing the automation loop from becoming entirely passive.

## **5\. Yield Calculation: Deconstructing the Margin (Price) Algorithms**

The JavaScript function actualDealerPrice(dealer) serves as the primary computational engine for calculating localized revenue generation within the application's $digest cycle.3 To import this specific mechanic accurately into an independent engine, it is necessary to formally reconstruct how the base values of commodities interact mathematically with the specific, star-derived margin profile of the assigned automation unit.

### **5.1 Base Commodity Valuation and the Problem of Ludo-Narrative Dissonance**

Every tradeable commodity object instantiated within the application possesses an intrinsic, hard-coded base monetary value. However, a deep analysis of user feedback regarding the game's economic balance reveals a significant structural discrepancy between real-world physical commodity valuation and the game's required mathematical scaling—a well-documented phenomenon in game design known as ludo-narrative dissonance.2

Specifically, users have highlighted that measuring extremely potent chemical compounds, such as lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD), by the "gram" utilizing the exact same physical metrics applied to bulk agricultural products like cannabis creates a massive economic distortion.2 In physical reality, a single gram of LSD translates to millions of active microgram doses, equating to tens of thousands of dollars in baseline market value.2 To maintain an escalating gameplay balance and prevent the economy from breaking instantly upon the unlocking of higher-tier compounds, the simulation is forced to treat all starting commodities with somewhat generalized, progressively scaling base prices per standardized gram. The original architect acknowledged this discrepancy, noting the necessity of continuously adjusting the internal algorithms to eventually reflect realistic market density differences.1

### **5.2 Mathematical Formulation of the Margin Equation**

To reverse-engineer the precise mechanics requested—specifically determining how a one to five-star rating dictates the exact monetary yield of a single gram of material—one must model the implicit logic operating beneath the actualDealerPrice and getStars functions.3

The game relies on applying an escalating coefficient multiplier based on the abstract star rating to the intrinsic base value of the chosen commodity. Let ![][image1] represent the absolute baseline monetary value of a specific commodity category ![][image2] per standard unit of mass. Let ![][image3] represent the specific multiplier derived directly from the unit's abstract star rating ![][image4]. Let ![][image5] represent the aggregate sum of any persistent global multipliers or localized, purchased equipment upgrades specifically augmenting price yield.

The core yield calculation per individual unit processed is formulated as:

![][image6]  
Within the application's markup, the executed output of this specific formula is rendered dynamically within the obfuscated tooltip element: \<p class="jg-tooltip left-tip"\>{{dealer.name.split(' ')}} sells 1g of {{dealer.drug}} for {{actualDealerPrice(dealer)|money}}\</p\>.3 The interpolation filter, explicitly declared as |money, formats the raw floating-point calculation into a legible currency string complete with appropriate localized symbols.3

### **5.3 Extrapolating the Star Modifiers for External Implementation**

Because the exact variable array mapping stars to hardcode integers is shielded in the compiled application, an external developer must construct a structured matrix based on standard incremental progression logic to accurately recreate the economic sensation of the simulation. User context indicates that low-star units are immediately discarded, while five and six-star units are heavily protected, implying an exponential rather than linear value scale.1

The following logical matrix provides a highly functional, reconstructed blueprint for mapping abstract visual stars to specific mathematical multipliers (![][image7]) and concrete monetary outputs, assuming a theoretical baseline commodity where ![][image8] per gram:

| Visual Star Rating (S) | Margin Multiplier (Mmargin​) | Example Yield Calculation (Pbase​=$10) | Economic Utility Context within the Simulation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1 Star | 1.0x (Baseline) | $10.00 per gram | Absolute minimum viability; discarded immediately upon better rolls. |
| 2 Stars | 1.5x | $15.00 per gram | Entry-level margin capability; utilized only in the earliest progression stages. |
| 3 Stars | 2.5x | $25.00 per gram | Mid-tier standardization; highly stable economic foundation. |
| 4 Stars | 4.0x | $40.00 per gram | Advanced optimization; required to maximize the value of scarce late-game compounds. |
| 5 Stars | 7.5x | $75.00 per gram | Elite processing capability; the primary target of all late-game hiring cycles. |
| 6 Stars (Anomaly) | 15.0x | $150.00 per gram | Extreme statistical outlier; heavily protected by prestige retention mechanics.1 |

### **5.4 The Strategic Implementation of Margin Specialization**

Because the architectural framework of the application allows players to continuously and precisely dictate exactly which global commodity a specific unit is authorized to process, handled via the user interface element \<select class="form-control" ng-model="dealer.drug" ng-change="updateDealerDrugIndex()"\> 3, a highly advanced optimization strategy emerges organically.

Players must perpetually execute cross-referencing logic, evaluating the base value of newly unlocked, higher-tier production compounds against the margin multipliers of their existing workforce. If an advanced player unlocks a significantly higher-tier product, its inherent ![][image9] will naturally dwarf the baseline value of all previous early-game commodities. Reassigning a five-star or six-star Margin dealer exclusively to this newly unlocked product creates an immediate, exponential spike in the aggregate dealer.cashPerSecond output.3 Therefore, players are heavily incentivized to strictly reserve their highest-margin autonomous nodes exclusively for their most valuable, lowest-yield, and hardest-to-produce supply chains.

## **6\. Throughput Dynamics: Deconstructing the Volume Algorithms**

While the Margin equations dictate the absolute financial efficiency of a single transaction, the Volume equations strictly dictate the overarching velocity of the localized economy. The volume processing limitations within the application are calculated continuously via the function actualDealerVolume(dealer, dealer.drug).3

### **6.1 Primary Throughput Limitations and the Density Problem**

The absolute primary metric evaluated by the system is how much of the specific, assigned product the unit is capable of successfully removing from the global inventory per computational second. The application displays this critical throughput boundary via another localized tooltip element, stating: \<p class="jg-tooltip left-tip"\>... can sell up to {{actualDealerVolume(dealer,dealer.drug)|weight}} of {{dealer.drug}} per second...\</p\>.3

Initially, the simulation engine processed this throughput logic universally across all commodities. The developer explicitly stated that under early logic models, if a dealer possessed the intrinsic statistical capability to process four grams per second, that arbitrary numerical limit applied equally regardless of whether the product was a highly bulky organic plant matter or an ultra-dense, highly concentrated laboratory chemical.1 Recognizing the severe lack of environmental verisimilitude this created, algorithmic adjustments were proposed and necessitated to ensure that the volume capacity became a dynamic function reliant not merely on the unit's intrinsic statistics, but heavily modified by the physical density of the specific commodity being processed.1

To accurately replicate this advanced, density-aware iteration of the throughput calculation, the volume logic must be mathematically structured as follows. Let ![][image10] represent the dealer's core throughput capability derived directly from their visual Volume star rating. Let ![][image11] represent an arbitrary density coefficient assigned within the global database to commodity ![][image2]. For standard agricultural goods, ![][image12] might equal 1.0, whereas for hyper-dense synthetic compounds, it might equal 0.1, thereby artificially slowing the throughput of highly valuable materials.

The primary physical throughput capacity per second is thus expressed as:

![][image13]

### **6.2 Designing the Volume Star Scaling Matrix**

Similar to the margin scaling, the exact absolute values assigned to a one through five-star volume rating require structural mapping for integration into a new framework. Based on the developer's contextual note establishing a four-gram-per-second baseline for mid-tier throughput 1, we can construct a proportional matrix for ![][image10]:

| Visual Star Rating (S) | Base Volume Throughput (Vbase​) | Analytical Justification based on Source Mechanics |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1 Star | 1.0 grams / sec | Absolute minimum processing speed to ensure base functionality. |
| 2 Stars | 2.5 grams / sec | Minor escalation to keep pace with initial production upgrades. |
| 3 Stars | 4.0 grams / sec | The established baseline benchmark metric explicitly referenced by the system architect.1 |
| 4 Stars | 8.0 grams / sec | High-velocity processing required to clear accumulating mid-game bottlenecks. |
| 5 Stars | 15.0 grams / sec | Elite capacity strictly necessary to prevent complete stagnation in late-game infrastructures.1 |

### **6.3 Secondary Inventory Liquidation: The sideVolume Protocol**

One of the most mathematically sophisticated and vital mechanics uncovered deep within the application's exposed source code logic is the implementation of passive, secondary inventory liquidation, categorized and referred to internally as the dealer.sideVolume mechanic.3

In complex, multi-tiered factory and incremental simulation games, players inevitably end up generating massive, permanently stagnant stockpiles of early-game materials. For instance, a player might accumulate tens of thousands of grams of early-game agricultural products simply because all active, high-efficiency dealers have been logically reassigned to process much higher-tier synthetic chemicals.1 To prevent the user from being forced into the highly tedious administrative task of manually swapping elite dealers backward down the production chain merely to clear out legacy inventory—a deeply disruptive process that shatters optimal revenue streams—the application introduces an automated fractional bleed mechanic.1

The source codebase explicitly exposes this sophisticated mechanic in the presentation logic: ...as well as {{dealer.sideVolume \* actualDealerVolume(dealer,dealer.drug)|weight}} of other drugs.3

This formula proves that while an automation unit's primary processing capability is dedicated exclusively to their assigned, targeted product, they possess an inherent, passive, concurrent ability to liquidate fractional mathematical percentages of all unassigned global inventory.

For example, if the actualDealerVolume evaluates to ten grams per second, and the universal sideVolume structural coefficient is set within the global state to 0.10 (representing a ten percent fractional bleed), the unit will successfully process ten grams of their primary assigned target good. Simultaneously, without requiring any localized player input, that exact same unit will passively liquidate up to one gram per second of *every single other commodity currently existing within the player's overarching global stockpile*.

This specific mechanic serves as an incredibly vital automated economic exhaust valve. It completely guarantees that all legacy production chains, no matter how obsolete they become relative to the late-game economy, continue to provide a constant, reliable baseline trickle of passive capital generation without ever requiring direct administrative intervention from the player.

### **6.4 Calculating Total Global Economic Velocity**

Understanding the dichotomy between the primary active processing and the secondary passive bleed is essential for capturing the true financial output of an individual unit. The total economic velocity generated by an individual unit, which is subsequently aggregated and reflected in the user interface as dealer.cashPerSecond, is not a simple linear calculation. It is the aggregate sum of the primary throughput yield added to the combined sum of all active peripheral secondary yields.3

The complete, comprehensive mathematical model defining an individual unit's total revenue generation per computational tick must be formalized as:

![][image14]  
*(Where ![][image15] represents the entire array of all accumulated secondary commodities currently existing within the active, global inventory pipeline).*

## **7\. Progression Variables: Local Upgrades, Equipment Augmentation, and Silk Road Mechanics**

A completely static economic simulation inevitably leads to player fatigue. To circumvent this, the dealer mechanics within the application are heavily augmented by independent layers of continuous progression systems. These systems are divided into localized capital expenditure loops and massive, overarching macroeconomic paradigms.1

### **7.1 Localized Unit Upgrades and Equipment Scaling**

The application's logic framework features a robust upgrade array that is scoped exclusively to the individual automation unit object, structured via the directive ng-repeat="upgrade in dealer.upgrades | orderBy:'price' track by $index".3 Players are granted the ability to continuously siphon standard generated currency to purchase specialized equipment for specific units, fundamentally altering that specific unit's intrinsic baseline multipliers without altering the global state.

Community analytics indicate these localized upgrades are deeply themed around logistical and security parameters, including sequential tiers categorized conceptually as outfitting the unit with an "Armed Gang", a "Ferrari", or a "Copter".1 From a mathematical perspective, these specialized items function as sequential step-functions within the code. They apply either massive flat mathematical additions or expansive percentage scalars to the intrinsic margin and volume algorithms detailed in the prior sections. To ensure fluid user interaction, the user interface logic automatically and continuously evaluates the player's overarching global capital pool against the cost of these local upgrades. It dynamically updates the visual representation of the purchasing mechanism, utilizing conditional CSS classes such as ng-class="areDealerUpgradesAvailable(dealer)? 'icongreen' : 'iconred'" to provide immediate visual confirmation regarding localized purchasing power.3

### **7.2 Late-Game Macro-Systems: Automated Global Distribution**

Beyond localized individual upgrades, the application contains references to significant overarching paradigm shifts in how goods are distributed in the extreme late game. The source code contains explicit conditional flags such as ng-show="gameModel.silkRoadUnlocked &&\!options.autoSilk".3

This specific logic block indicates the existence of an advanced, unlockable global distribution network (conceptually mimicking the historical dark web marketplace). The presence of the options.autoSilk variable strongly implies that upon reaching a certain threshold of capital generation, the player can unlock a secondary layer of automation. This system likely supersedes the requirement for individual localized dealers entirely, automating the overarching sale process at a global, macro-level scale. This serves to continually escalate the scale of the economy, ensuring the engine remains functional and engaging even when individual dealer capacities have reached their absolute programmatic limits.3

## **8\. Meta-Progression Paradigms: Timelines, Prestige, and Data Retention**

To maintain user engagement over prolonged periods spanning hundreds of computational hours, the application relies heavily on an implementation of the most standard mechanic within the idle and incremental genre: the prestige system. This architectural concept requires the player to intentionally execute a controlled collapse of their current economic timeline. Upon activation, the software purges the standard operational arrays, resetting all accumulated cash, standard commodity pipelines, and baseline automation units to an absolute zero state, in exchange for the injection of permanent meta-progress multipliers.4

### **8.1 The Dual-Vector Prestige Architecture**

The specific prestige architecture governing this simulation operates effectively on a highly structured dual-vector implementation framework:

1. **Meta-Currency Generation and The Respect Economy:** The simulation introduces a completely separate, secondary computational currency, designated internally as "Respect".4 Unlike standard capital, which is generated linearly via commodity liquidation, Respect is generated completely asynchronously over vast periods of time. The player accumulates this currency by utilizing capital to hire a specialized, distinct class of unit categorized as "muscle".4 Upon executing the timeline reset and collapsing the primary economy, the accumulated pool of Respect points is permanently preserved. In the newly generated timeline, these points are injected into permanent global multipliers, allowing the player to purchase structural advantages such as expanding overall market territory or mathematically negotiating permanent percentage reductions in all baseline infrastructural costs.4  
2. **Asset Retention and The Elite Captain Protocol:** Executing a hard reset inherently causes extreme psychological friction for the user, primarily due to the permanent destruction of highly optimized, perfectly rolled automation nodes that may have taken hours of active churning to discover. To directly mitigate this user frustration, the application implements a highly specific retention protocol known as the "Captain" mechanic.4 A Captain is a user-designated elite unit whose underlying statistical array—specifically their perfect originalVolume and originalPrice integer values—as well as their heavily invested local equipment array, are completely flagged to bypass the destructive data purge of the timeline reset.4 These designated objects become permanent, indestructible fixtures attached directly to the player's overarching save profile. Consequently, they provide a massive, immediate baseline throughput and margin advantage at the very inception of a newly instantiated timeline, drastically accelerating the early-game progression loop.4

## **9\. Structural Frameworks for Independent Proprietary Integration**

For software engineers and game designers seeking to extract the absolute essence of these mechanics and inject them flawlessly into a new, independent proprietary game engine (such as Unity, Unreal, or custom web frameworks), the application's unique variables must be normalized into an agnostic, object-oriented design architecture. The following structured blueprint provides the necessary foundation for reverse-engineering the exact mechanical loop.

### **9.1 Required Database Schemas and Object Instantiation**

The primary object classes strictly required to perfectly recreate this complex economic ecosystem are the Commodity class, the AutomationUnit class, and the centralized MacroState controller.

The Commodity data structure must contain:

* A unique string identifier parameter.  
* A string parameter for interface rendering.  
* A critical float parameter representing the intrinsic baseline monetary yield generated per standard physical unit of measurement.  
* A density coefficient float (ranging logically from 0.1 to 1.0) strictly dictating how rapidly this specific physical material can be processed relative to standard volume processing limitations.  
* An integer parameter tracking current, globally available accumulated mass.  
* A float parameter tracking the current mathematical generation rate based entirely on facility upgrades.

The AutomationUnit data structure (representing the localized dealer) must contain:

* A unique cryptographic hash identifier.  
* A procedurally generated string name for interface presentation.  
* An active pointer parameter linking the specific unit instance to a localized product pipeline.  
* An integer parameter representing visual competency in pricing efficiency (the Margin Star).  
* An integer parameter representing visual competency in logistical capability (the Volume Star).  
* A derived float parameter explicitly calculating the Margin multiplier scaling.  
* A derived float parameter explicitly calculating the Volume throughput capacity scaling.  
* A universally applied global float coefficient dictating the exact fractional percentage utilized for passive, secondary liquidation loops.  
* A boolean flag explicitly protecting the specific object instance from execution during automated garbage collection and prestige timeline resets.  
* A mutable boolean status flag that dynamically toggles to false during the execution of randomized environmental disruption algorithms.

### **9.2 Execution of the Core Loop Cycle**

To ensure economic stability, the foundational logic must be executed sequentially and continuously on a standardized temporal tick, universally processed every physical second or evaluated fractionally per rendered frame based on engine delta times.

The execution must begin with a dedicated Production Phase. The engine iterates linearly through all instantiated Commodity objects, continuously increasing the total available inventory mass by the algorithmically determined production generation per second value.

Following production, the engine initiates the vital Calculation Phase. The loop iterates through all active AutomationUnit objects currently holding an active status flag. It first determines the assigned primary product pipeline. It mathematically calculates the absolute primary volume throughput capacity by multiplying the base unit capacity by the specific commodity density factor and all active local equipment upgrades. Simultaneously, it calculates the localized primary price yield by multiplying the core commodity baseline value by the unit's intrinsic margin multiplier. The engine must explicitly check the total available inventory. The unit is only authorized to mathematically process the lesser amount of either its absolute maximum volume capacity or the physically available global inventory. The successfully processed amount is systematically deducted from the global database, and the resultant revenue vector is injected directly back into the overarching global capital pool.

Subsequently, the engine executes the Secondary Exhaust Phase for that exact same unit object. It iterates through every other commodity currently stored in the global database. It calculates the fractional secondary volume utilizing the side volume coefficient. It processes this fractional amount, calculates the secondary price yields based entirely on the base values of the peripheral commodities, and injects that resultant capital directly into the primary economic pool.

Finally, the engine synchronizes the Document Object Model, triggering conditional UI warning flags if the total facility generation rate has mathematically eclipsed the total localized volume capacities, forcing the player back into the interactive human-resource management loop.

## **10\. Analytical Syntheses and Design Methodologies**

The automated resource processing systems designed within this specific application structure represent a highly sophisticated, mathematically elegant solution to the inherent problem of resource liquidation within infinitely scaling incremental economies. By deliberately shifting the mechanical focus away from direct active mechanics and completely embracing an abstracted human-resource management paradigm, the simulation introduces complex, multivariate logistical problem-solving into what is traditionally a linear, predictable genre of game design.

The profound ingenuity of this specific architecture lies entirely in the calculated, deliberate mechanical friction generated between its two fundamental statistical pillars: absolute Margin efficiency and maximum logistical Volume. The mathematical relationship explicitly defined between the production scalar values and the liquidation variables ensures that the simulated ecosystem is structurally designed to never rest in a state of permanent static equilibrium. The player is continuously and deliberately subjected to rotating supply chain bottlenecks that demand administrative attention and strategic restructuring.

Furthermore, the sophisticated application of progressive data disclosure—intentionally hiding highly complex mathematical float outputs beneath easily parsed visual heuristics like a simple five-star rating—allows the user interface to remain accessible while retaining vast algorithmic depth. By rigorously adopting the architectural schemas, execution loops, density modifiers, and fractional inventory bleeding algorithms detailed comprehensively throughout this analysis, developers can successfully reconstruct and implement the highly engaging, continuously oscillating economic pressures that fundamentally define this framework into any independent interactive ecosystem.

#### **Works cited**

1.
